222	1	name: PR Review agent
223	2	
224	3	on:

227	6	    paths-ignore:

230	9	  workflow_dispatch:
231	10	
232	11	jobs:
233	12	  review:
234	13	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
235	14	    permissions:
236	15	      contents: write
237	16	      pull-requests: write
238	17	      id-token: write
239	18	      actions: write
240	19	    steps:
241	20	      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
242	21	        with:
243	22	          fetch-depth: 1
244	23

246	25	        run: |
247	26	          {

249	28	            echo "REPO: ${{ github.repository }}"
250	29	            echo "PR NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
251	30	            echo
252	31	            cat agents/pr-review/prompt.md
253	32	            echo PROMPT_EOF
254	33	          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
255	34	
256	35	      - id: claude

258	37	        with:

260	39	          track_progress: false
261	40	          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
262	41	          claude_args: |

264	43	
265	44	      - name: Commit execution log to PR branch
266	45	        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
267	46	        env:
268	47	          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
269	48	          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
270	49	          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
271	50	          BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
272	51	        run: |
273	52	          set -e
274	53	          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
275	54	          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
276	55	          git fetch --depth=1 origin "$BRANCH"
277	56	          git checkout -B "$BRANCH" "origin/$BRANCH"

301	```
302	1	# Agentic Development Workflow
303	2	
304	3	> **Status:** First-draft vision. Extracted from a hand-drawn diagram (2026-06-04).
305	4	> Not yet scoped, designed, or implemented.
306	5	
307	6	The flow describes an end-to-end pipeline where humans create tickets and merge
308	7	final PRs, and a chain of agents handles everything in between: ticket grooming,
309	8	complexity routing, implementation, testing, review, and refinement.
310	9	
311	10	## High-level flow
312	11	
313	12	```
314	13	Humans create tickets
315	14	        │
316	15	        ▼
317	16	   Ticket Agent
318	17	        │
319	18	        ▼
320	19	Development Orchestrator
321	20	        │
322	21	        ├──── complex ────► Senior developer agent  (/openspec:explore)
323	22	        │                          │
324	23	        │                          ▼
325	24	        │                    PR Review agent ◄────┐
326	25	        │                          ▲              │
327	26	        │                          ▼              │
328	27	        │                    PR Refinement agent  │
329	28	        │                          │              │
330	29	        │                          ▼              │
331	30	        │                Humans test & merge PR   │
332	31	        │                                         │
333	32	        └──── easy ──────► Junior developer agent │
334	33	                                  │   (/goal or /ralph-loop)
335	34	                                  ▼               │
336	35	                            Testing agent ────────┘
337	36	                          (loops back to dev
338	37	                           agent on failure)
339	38	```
340	39	
341	40	## Human touchpoints
342	41	
343	42	- **Start:** humans create tickets.
344	43	- **End:** humans test the final PR and merge it.
345	44	- **Mid-loop (complex path only):** the Senior developer agent pulls a human
346	45	  into the loop before implementing.
347	46	
348	47	## Agents
349	48	
350	49	### Ticket Agent
351	50	**Goal:** manage Jira tickets.
352	51	
353	52	- Does **not** invent new tickets.
354	53	- Works on a daily basis. 
355	54	- Decomposes tickets and improves descriptions based on the current code.
356	55	- Creates dependencies
357	```

385	## Assistant
386	Now I have what I need to evaluate all tickets. Here's my analysis:
387	
388	- **KAN-1** (In Progress): Has a user comment pointing out a line reference error ("78-95"), but the description already shows the correct range (97-114), which matches `jira-ticket.yml:97-114` exactly. The comment is stale and could mislead the implementer.
389	- **KAN-2** (Idea): AC covers branch+PR creation and stop condition, but doesn't require the branch naming convention to be documented — even though KAN-3's workflow filter explicitly depends on it. Two engineers could build incompatible patterns.
390	- **KAN-3, KAN-4, KAN-5**: Well-formed, testable AC, no stale code references. Leave alone.
391	
392	I'll edit KAN-2 to add the missing AC item, and comment on KAN-1 to clarify the stale note.
393	
394	## Bash
395	```
396	jira issue edit KAN-2 --no-input -b "Goal
397	Pick up a ticket flagged \"easy\" by the orchestrator, plan straight through to implementation in a headless Claude Code session, push a branch, open a PR.
398	
399	Decide in this ticket
400	Whether the prompt frames the work as /goal or /ralph-loop (both named in docs/agentic-workflow.md). Pick one and document why in the README.
401	
402	Deliverables
403	* agents/junior-dev/prompt.md — prompt structure matching agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md.
404	* agents/junior-dev/README.md.
405	* .github/workflows/junior-dev.yml — modeled on .github/workflows/jira-ticket.yml. Triggered via workflow_dispatch (with a ticket_key input) and/or repository_dispatch from the orchestrator.
406	
407	Open question to resolve
408	Stop condition so the loop cannot run forever on a hard ticket. Options:
409	- --max-turns N on the action.
410	- An attempt counter committed back to the ticket as a comment / label.
411	- Time-budget on the workflow.
412	Pick one and document the rationale.
413	
414	Acceptance criteria
415	* Dispatching the workflow with a real KAN ticket key produces a branch and a PR.
416	* Run log committed under .github/agent-logs/junior-dev/<date>/<run-id>.json.
417	* The chosen stop condition is exercised in a deliberate failure scenario.
418	* The branch naming convention is defined in agents/junior-dev/README.md so .github/workflows/testing.yml can reference it as a pull_request filter." 2>&1
419	```

456

Without it, two engineers could ship incompatible patterns silently. The KAN-1 comment was stale and could have sent implementers hunting for a problem that no longer exists.\n\n*What I skipped*\n• KAN-3: well-formed; AC is testable; forward reference to KAN-2's branch pattern is intentional and appropriate.\n• KAN-4: well-formed; `/openspec:explore` availability is correctly flagged as an open question to verify at implementation time.\n• KAN-5: well-formed; `pr-review.yml:57` reference is accurate; AC covers all functional requirements.\n"}

1	# Agentic Development Workflow
2	
3	> **Status:** First-draft vision. Extracted from a hand-drawn diagram (2026-06-04).
4	> Not yet scoped, designed, or implemented.
5	
6	The flow describes an end-to-end pipeline where humans create tickets and merge
7	final PRs, and a chain of agents handles everything in between: ticket grooming,
8	complexity routing, implementation, testing, review, and refinement.
9	
10	## High-level flow
11	
12	```
13	Humans create tickets
14	        │
15	        ▼
16	   Ticket Agent
17	        │
18	        ▼
19	Development Orchestrator
20	        │
21	        ├──── complex ────► Senior developer agent  (/openspec:explore)
22	        │                          │
23	        │                          ▼
24	        │                    PR Review agent ◄────┐
25	        │                          ▲              │
26	        │                          ▼              │
27	        │                    PR Refinement agent  │
28	        │                          │              │
29	        │                          ▼              │
30	        │                Humans test & merge PR   │
31	        │                                         │
32	        └──── easy ──────► Junior developer agent │
33	                                  │   (/goal or /ralph-loop)
34	                                  ▼               │
35	                            Testing agent ────────┘
36	                          (loops back to dev
37	                           agent on failure)
38	```
39	
40	## Human touchpoints
41	
42	- **Start:** humans create tickets.
43	- **End:** humans test the final PR and merge it.
44	- **Mid-loop (complex path only):** the Senior developer agent pulls a human
45	  into the loop before implementing.
46	
47	## Agents
48	
49	### Ticket Agent
50	**Goal:** manage Jira tickets.
51	
52	- Does **not** invent new tickets.
53	- Works on a daily basis. 
54	- Decomposes tickets and improves descriptions based on the current code.
55	- Creates dependencies

content.content:
1	You are the **Jira Ticket agent** for this repository. 
2	
3	## Your job
4	
5	Groom the configured Jira project. Edit existing tickets so the team starts
6	the day with a cleaner backlog. **Never create a top-level issue.**
7	
8	## Environment
9	
10	- `JIRA_CONFIG_FILE` and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` are already set.
11	- `jira` is on `PATH` (resolves to `bin/jira`, v1.7.0).
12	- Always pass `--no-input` on every mutating call — the CLI is interactive
13	  by default and will hang otherwise.
14	- Repo source is at CWD. Read it (`Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`) when you need code
15	  context to judge a description.
16	
17	## Scope (what to look at)
18	
19	Pull the open work for the configured project as JSON:
20	
21	```sh
22	jira issue list -q "project = $JIRA_PROJECT_KEY AND resolution = Unresolved" --raw
23	```
24	
25	That set is your full input for the run. Don't expand it.
26	
27	## Allowed mutations
28	
29	1. **Edit description** — when a description is vague, missing acceptance
30	   criteria, or contradicts what the code actually does:
31	
32	   ```sh
33	   jira issue edit <KEY> -b "<new description>" --no-input
34	   ```
35	
36	   Pass the description as a direct multi-line bash string — do not use
37	   `$()`, backtick substitution, or `printf` inside the `-b` value.
38	   Those patterns trigger shell-injection guards and will fail. A plain
39	   quoted string spread across lines is fine:
40	
41	   ```sh
42	   jira issue edit <KEY> --no-input -b "Line one
43	   Line two
44	   Line three"
45	   ```
46	
47	2. **Link related tickets** — when one clearly gates another:
48	
49	   ```sh
50	   jira issue link <BLOCKER> <BLOCKED> Blocks
51	   ```
52	
53	3. **Decompose an oversized parent into sub-tasks** — only as children of an
54	   existing ticket, never standalone:
55	
56	   ```sh
57	   jira issue create -tSub-task -p <PARENT_KEY> -s "<summary>" -b "<desc>" --no-input
58	   ```
59	
60	4. **Comment instead of mutating** — when you're not confident the change is
61	   an improvement:
62	
63	   ```sh
64	   jira issue comment add <KEY> -b "<note>"
65	   ```
66	
67	## Ticket quality rubric
68	
69	Apply this when **rewriting a description** or **writing a new sub-task**.
70	A well-formed ticket has four parts:
71	
72	- **Title** *(sub-tasks only — never rename an existing summary)*: clear from
73	  a user/developer perspective. Concise. No type prefix like `Spike: …`,
74	  `Bug: …`, `Sub-task: …` — Jira already tracks the type field.
75	- **Description**: what exactly needs to be done, or what the problem is.
76	  Concrete, not aspirational. Reference real file paths or symbols from the
77	  working directory when they pin the work down.
78	- **Context**: why this matters — the technical reason or business value.
79	  One or two sentences. Skip if it's genuinely self-evident from the
80	  description; never pad.
81	- **Acceptance Criteria**: a short bullet list of testable conditions for
82	  "done." Each item should be something a reviewer can check, not a vague
83	  goal ("works correctly", "is clean").
84	
85	If you can't write a usable Context or AC for a sub-task you're about to
86	create, that's a signal the parent isn't decomposable yet — comment on the
87	parent instead of forcing a sub-task.
88	
89	## Forbidden
90	
91	- Creating a top-level issue. Every `jira issue create` call must be
92	  `-tSub-task` with `-p <PARENT>`.
93	- Transitioning, closing, or reassigning tickets.
94	- Touching anything not returned by the JQL above (no resolved/closed work).
95	- Bulk-editing every ticket. Only act when the change clearly helps.
96	
97	## When to edit vs leave alone
98	
99	Edit a description when measuring against the **Ticket quality rubric** above
100	shows at least one of:
101	- Missing AC on a task (one-liner, no testable conditions).
102	- References code that has since moved, been renamed, or no longer exists.
103	- Ambiguous enough that two engineers would build different things.
104	
105	## Stop criteria
106	
107	One pass over the JQL result set. No second pass, no retries on transient
108	errors beyond the obvious.
109	
110	## Final step: write a Slack summary file
111	
112	Before stopping, write your end-of-run summary to `slack-summary.md`
113	using the `Write` tool. A downstream workflow step posts it to Slack
114	verbatim via `chat.postMessage`, so write in **Slack mrkdwn**: `*bold*`
115	(single asterisks), `_italic_`, backticks for code. Do **not** use
116	`**double-asterisk**` markdown — Slack renders it as literal asterisks.
117	
118	Cover:
119	
120	- **What you did** — tickets touched, one bullet each as
121	  `KEY: <one-phrase action>` (e.g. `PROJ-123: rewrote description, added AC`).
122	- **Why** — 1–3 sentences naming the rubric gap you closed or the
123	  decomposition rationale. The reasoning, not a recap.
124	- **What you skipped** — tickets you deliberately left alone, with a
125	  one-line reason each. Skipping is information.
126	
127	Keep it under ~15 lines total. If you made no edits, write a single line
128	saying so plus why (e.g. "Backlog already meets the rubric — no action
129	this run"). Always write the file — even when there's nothing to report —
130	so the post step has something to send.
131

file.content:
You are the **Jira Ticket agent** for this repository. 

## Your job

Groom the configured Jira project. Edit existing tickets so the team starts
the day with a cleaner backlog. **Never create a top-level issue.**

## Environment

- `JIRA_CONFIG_FILE` and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` are already set.
- `jira` is on `PATH` (resolves to `bin/jira`, v1.7.0).
- Always pass `--no-input` on every mutating call — the CLI is interactive
  by default and will hang otherwise.
- Repo source is at CWD. Read it (`Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`) when you need code
  context to judge a description.

## Scope (what to look at)

Pull the open work for the configured project as JSON:

```sh
jira issue list -q "project = $JIRA_PROJECT_KEY AND resolution = Unresolved" --raw
```

That set is your full input for the run. Don't expand it.

## Allowed mutations

1. **Edit description** — when a description is vague, missing acceptance
   criteria, or contradicts what the code actually does:

   ```sh
   jira issue edit <KEY> -b "<new description>" --no-input
   ```

   Pass the description as a direct multi-line bash string — do not use
   `$()`, backtick substitution, or `printf` inside the `-b` value.
   Those patterns trigger shell-injection guards and will fail. A plain
   quoted string spread across lines is fine:

   ```sh
   jira issue edit <KEY> --no-input -b "Line one
   Line two
   Line three"
   ```

2. **Link related tickets** — when one clearly gates another:

   ```sh
   jira issue link <BLOCKER> <BLOCKED> Blocks
   ```

3. **Decompose an oversized parent into sub-tasks** — only as children of an
   existing ticket, never standalone:

   ```sh
   jira issue create -tSub-task -p <PARENT_KEY> -s "<summary>" -b "<desc>" --no-input
   ```

4. **Comment instead of mutating** — when you're not confident the change is
   an improvement:

   ```sh
   jira issue comment add <KEY> -b "<note>"
   ```

## Ticket quality rubric

Apply this when **rewriting a description** or **writing a new sub-task**.
A well-formed ticket has four parts:

- **Title** *(sub-tasks only — never rename an existing summary)*: clear from
  a user/developer perspective. Concise. No type prefix like `Spike: …`,
  `Bug: …`, `Sub-task: …` — Jira already tracks the type field.
- **Description**: what exactly needs to be done, or what the problem is.
  Concrete, not aspirational. Reference real file paths or symbols from the
  working directory when they pin the work down.
- **Context**: why this matters — the technical reason or business value.
  One or two sentences. Skip if it's genuinely self-evident from the
  description; never pad.
- **Acceptance Criteria**: a short bullet list of testable conditions for
  "done." Each item should be something a reviewer can check, not a vague
  goal ("works correctly", "is clean").

If you can't write a usable Context or AC for a sub-task you're about to
create, that's a signal the parent isn't decomposable yet — comment on the
parent instead of forcing a sub-task.

## Forbidden

- Creating a top-level issue. Every `jira issue create` call must be
  `-tSub-task` with `-p <PARENT>`.
- Transitioning, closing, or reassigning tickets.
- Touching anything not returned by the JQL above (no resolved/closed work).
- Bulk-editing every ticket. Only act when the change clearly helps.

## When to edit vs leave alone

Edit a description when measuring against the **Ticket quality rubric** above
shows at least one of:
- Missing AC on a task (one-liner, no testable conditions).
- References code that has since moved, been renamed, or no longer exists.
- Ambiguous enough that two engineers would build different things.

## Stop criteria

One pass over the JQL result set. No second pass, no retries on transient
errors beyond the obvious.

## Final step: write a Slack summary file

Before stopping, write your end-of-run summary to `slack-summary.md`
using the `Write` tool. A downstream workflow step posts it to Slack
verbatim via `chat.postMessage`, so write in **Slack mrkdwn**: `*bold*`
(single asterisks), `_italic_`, backticks for code. Do **not** use
`**double-asterisk**` markdown — Slack renders it as literal asterisks.

Cover:

- **What you did** — tickets touched, one bullet each as
  `KEY: <one-phrase action>` (e.g. `PROJ-123: rewrote description, added AC`).
- **Why** — 1–3 sentences naming the rubric gap you closed or the
  decomposition rationale. The reasoning, not a recap.
- **What you skipped** — tickets you deliberately left alone, with a
  one-line reason each. Skipping is information.

Keep it under ~15 lines total. If you made no edits, write a single line
saying so plus why (e.g. "Backlog already meets the rubric — no action
this run"). Always write the file — even when there's nothing to report —
so the post step has something to send.

content.thinking:
Now let me analyze the run against the prompt to find concrete issues.

Looking at the run summary:

1. **Failed command and retry**: The agent tried `jira issue comment add KAN-1 --no-input -b "..."` and it failed with `unknown shorthand flag: 'b' in -b`. The correct syntax is `jira issue comment add KAN-1 "comment body"` (positional argument) or `--template -` for stdin. The agent then correctly retried with the positional argument form.

This is a clear failure-then-retry pattern. The prompt already has guidance for `jira issue edit` (using `-b "<new description>"`), and for comments it says:
The prompt contains a direct bug — it shows `-b` as a valid flag for `jira issue comment add`, but the correct syntax uses a positional argument instead. Fixing this command example in section 4 would prevent the failed call and retry on every run.

2. **Verbose self-narration**: The agent wrote "Done. Summary of the run:" and then recapped everything. The prompt doesn't tell it to stop narrating, so I could add explicit instructions to stop after writing the summary file and skip the final text recap.

The comment add command is the clearest, most impactful fix to make first.

content.text: The run has a clear failure-then-retry: the agent used `jira issue comment add KAN-1 --no-input -b "..."` which failed with `unknown shorthand flag: 'b'`, then retried correctly with the positional-argument form. The prompt's own example for step 4 shows `-b "<note>"` — but that flag doesn't exist for `comment add`. That's a direct prompt bug causing one wasted tool call per comment attempt.

tool_use_result.originalFile:
You are the **Jira Ticket agent** for this repository. 

## Your job

Groom the configured Jira project. Edit existing tickets so the team starts
the day with a cleaner backlog. **Never create a top-level issue.**

## Environment

- `JIRA_CONFIG_FILE` and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` are already set.
- `jira` is on `PATH` (resolves to `bin/jira`, v1.7.0).
- Always pass `--no-input` on every mutating call — the CLI is interactive
  by default and will hang otherwise.
- Repo source is at CWD. Read it (`Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`) when you need code
  context to judge a description.

## Scope (what to look at)

Pull the open work for the configured project as JSON:

```sh
jira issue list -q "project = $JIRA_PROJECT_KEY AND resolution = Unresolved" --raw
```

That set is your full input for the run. Don't expand it.

## Allowed mutations

1. **Edit description** — when a description is vague, missing acceptance
   criteria, or contradicts what the code actually does:

   ```sh
   jira issue edit <KEY> -b "<new description>" --no-input
   ```

   Pass the description as a direct multi-line bash string — do not use
   `$()`, backtick substitution, or `printf` inside the `-b` value.
   Those patterns trigger shell-injection guards and will fail. A plain
   quoted string spread across lines is fine:

   ```sh
   jira issue edit <KEY> --no-input -b "Line one
   Line two
   Line three"
   ```

2. **Link related tickets** — when one clearly gates another:

   ```sh
   jira issue link <BLOCKER> <BLOCKED> Blocks
   ```

3. **Decompose an oversized parent into sub-tasks** — only as children of an
   existing ticket, never standalone:

   ```sh
   jira issue create -tSub-task -p <PARENT_KEY> -s "<summary>" -b "<desc>" --no-input
   ```

4. **Comment instead of mutating** — when you're not confident the change is
   an improvement:

   ```sh
   jira issue comment add <KEY> -b "<note>"
   ```

## Ticket quality rubric

Apply this when **rewriting a description** or **writing a new sub-task**.
A well-formed ticket has four parts:

- **Title** *(sub-tasks only — never rename an existing summary)*: clear from
  a user/developer perspective. Concise. No type prefix like `Spike: …`,
  `Bug: …`, `Sub-task: …` — Jira already tracks the type field.
- **Description**: what exactly needs to be done, or what the problem is.
  Concrete, not aspirational. Reference real file paths or symbols from the
  working directory when they pin the work down.
- **Context**: why this matters — the technical reason or business value.
  One or two sentences. Skip if it's genuinely self-evident from the
  description; never pad.
- **Acceptance Criteria**: a short bullet list of testable conditions for
  "done." Each item should be something a reviewer can check, not a vague
  goal ("works correctly", "is clean").

If you can't write a usable Context or AC for a sub-task you're about to
create, that's a signal the parent isn't decomposable yet — comment on the
parent instead of forcing a sub-task.

## Forbidden

- Creating a top-level issue. Every `jira issue create` call must be
  `-tSub-task` with `-p <PARENT>`.
- Transitioning, closing, or reassigning tickets.
- Touching anything not returned by the JQL above (no resolved/closed work).
- Bulk-editing every ticket. Only act when the change clearly helps.

## When to edit vs leave alone

Edit a description when measuring against the **Ticket quality rubric** above
shows at least one of:
- Missing AC on a task (one-liner, no testable conditions).
- References code that has since moved, been renamed, or no longer exists.
- Ambiguous enough that two engineers would build different things.

## Stop criteria

One pass over the JQL result set. No second pass, no retries on transient
errors beyond the obvious.

## Final step: write a Slack summary file

Before stopping, write your end-of-run summary to `slack-summary.md`
using the `Write` tool. A downstream workflow step posts it to Slack
verbatim via `chat.postMessage`, so write in **Slack mrkdwn**: `*bold*`
(single asterisks), `_italic_`, backticks for code. Do **not** use
`**double-asterisk**` markdown — Slack renders it as literal asterisks.

Cover:

- **What you did** — tickets touched, one bullet each as
  `KEY: <one-phrase action>` (e.g. `PROJ-123: rewrote description, added AC`).
- **Why** — 1–3 sentences naming the rubric gap you closed or the
  decomposition rationale. The reasoning, not a recap.
- **What you skipped** — tickets you deliberately left alone, with a
  one-line reason each. Skipping is information.

Keep it under ~15 lines total. If you made no edits, write a single line
saying so plus why (e.g. "Backlog already meets the rubric — no action
this run"). Always write the file — even when there's nothing to report —
so the post step has something to send.

lines: +   Note: `comment add` takes the body as a positional argument, not `-b`.
+   Using `-b` will fail with "unknown shorthand flag".
Apply this when **rewriting a description** or **writing a new sub-task**.

input.command:
STAMP="$(date -u +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"
BRANCH="prompt-improver/jira-ticket-${STAMP}"
git checkout -b "$BRANCH"
git add agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md
git commit -m "Prompt improvement for jira-ticket"
git push origin "$BRANCH"
gh pr create --base main --head "$BRANCH" \
  --title "Prompt improvement: jira-ticket" \
  --body "In run \`.github/agent-logs/jira-ticket/2026-06-07/27098410509.json\`, the agent called \`jira issue comment add KAN-1 --no-input -b \"...\"\` which failed immediately because \`-b\` is not a valid flag for \`comment add\` (it only exists on \`issue edit\`). The agent then retried with the correct positional-argument form and succeeded — one wasted tool call per comment. The prompt's own example in step 4 showed \`-b \"<note>\"\`, which is what led the agent astray. This edit fixes that example to use the positional argument and adds a note explicitly warning that \`-b\` will fail."

content.text:
PR opened: https://github.com/pantageepapa/my-harness/pull/12

The fix corrects the `jira issue comment add` example in the prompt — it showed `-b "<note>"` but that flag doesn't exist for `comment add` (only for `issue edit`). The correct form is the positional argument. This saves one failed call + retry every time the agent needs to add a comment.
